{{Cite sources|date=2018-08-30}} {{Page number|date=2018-08-30}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name=Kumomi Sengen Shrine | image=[[File:Kumomi-Sengen-jinja (Honden).jpg|250px|Main Hall of Kumomi Sengen Shrine (Photographed on February 11, 2017)]]
Main Hall of Kumomi Sengen Shrine
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|coord={{coord|34|43|31.7|N|138|44|24.5|E}}}} | location=[[Shizuoka Prefecture]][[Kamo District, Shizuoka|Kamo District]][[Matsuzaki, Shizuoka|Matsuzaki Town]] Kumomi 386-2 | Latitude = 34 | Latitude_min = 43 | Latitude_sec = 31.7 | N/S = N | Longitude = 138 | Longitude_min = 44 | Longitude_sec = 24.5 | E/W = E | ISO = JP-22 | Map = Japan Shizuoka | Label Position = left | deity=[[Iwanaga-hime|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto]] | Shrine Rank=[[Gōsha]], (Traditionally) [[Shikinaisha]] | Established=Unknown | Annual Festival=[[July 14]], [[July 15]], [[July 16]] }} '''Kumomi Sengen Shrine''' (雲見浅間神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located on {{ill|Mount Eboshi (Matsuzaki, Shizuoka)|ja|烏帽子山 (松崎町)|12=draft}}, with an elevation of 162 meters, in {{ill|Kumomi Onsen|ja|雲見温泉|12=draft}}, {{ill|Matsuzaki, Shizuoka|ja|松崎町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県|12=draft}}, Japan. It is also known as '''Kumomi Shrine''' (雲見神社) or '''Ontake Sengen Shrine''' (御嶽浅間宮). It is one of the rare [[Sengen shrine|Asama Shrines]] dedicated solely to {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|lt=Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫尊|12=draft}}. == Overview == Mount Eboshi, also called Ontake (御嶽山) or Kumomi-ga-take (雲見ヶ嶽), is a stand-alone {{ill|Tuff|ja|凝灰岩|12=draft}} rock formation located at the westernmost coast of the Izu Peninsula. The shrine's main hall is located just below the summit, while the worship hall and the middle shrine (Nakanomiya) are on the mountain's slopes. Historically, the shrine was off-limits to women, who were only allowed to worship at the middle shrine. From the base at {{ill|National Route 136 (Japan)|ja|国道136号|12=draft}}, the shrine is accessible by a steep 25-minute climb, passing two worship halls before reaching the main shrine. The 428-step stone stairway is extremely steep and includes sections with unstable footing. Due to strong winds near the summit, visitors require sturdy footwear, and light clothing such as high heels is strongly discouraged. Kumomi-Sengen-jinja (Honden s3).jpg|Honden Kumomi-Sengen-jinja (Haiden s2).jpg|Haiden Kumomi-Sengen-jinja (Nakanomiya).jpg|Nakanomiya Meotomatsu Kumomi-Sengen-jinja.jpg|Sacred [[pine tree]] known as "Meoto Matsu" == History == The founding date and origins are unknown. It is traditionally identified with "Ishito no Myojin," listed as "Conferred Fourth Rank, Upper Grade" in the ''Izu Province Shrine Ranking Book,'' and believed to correspond to "Iwanohime no Mikoto Shrine" in the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho,'' although conclusive evidence is lacking. According to the ''Zushu Shiko,'' the name "Ishito no Myojin" derives from a stone gate called "Asama-mon" in the sea, likely referring to the '''Senganmon''' rock formation about 800 meters south of Mount Eboshi. Shrine lore recounts that the main hall was swept into the sea by a typhoon and rebuilt in [[1657]] (Meireki 3). The shrine was a center for [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism) and visited by ascetics until the [[Meiji Restoration]] when it was designated as a [[Gōsha]] following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism. After the abolition of the shrine ranking system post-[[World War II]], local residents have maintained the shrine. == Legends == {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|lt=Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫尊|12=draft}}, the enshrined deity, is the sister of {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花咲耶姫命|12=draft}}, the deity of [[Mount Fuji]] and the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|Fujisan Hongŭ Sengen Taisha]], and daughter of {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山祗命|1=Ōyamatsumi|12=draft}}. In mythology, Iwanaga-hime was considered unattractive but promised longevity like a rock to her offspring, while her beautiful sister Konohanasakuya-hime’s children were destined for short lives like flowers. The heavenly grandson {{ill|Ninigi|ja|瓊瓊杵命|12=draft}} rejected Iwanaga-hime, causing her to retreat to Kumomi, leading to enmity between the once-close sisters. According to {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長|12=draft}}'s ''Kojikiden,'' the jealous Iwanaga-hime fled to Kumomi, where she was enshrined on a small rocky mountain, while her kind sister stretched tall to look for her, becoming the towering Mount Fuji. Local legends say that when Mount Eboshi is clear, Mount Fuji is shrouded in clouds, and vice versa. Praising Mount Fuji from Mount Eboshi is said to invite misfortune, and "Fuji" was once taboo in local speech. == References == * ''Izu Encyclopedia,'' edited by the Izu Studies Association, Hagoromo Publishing, 2010. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == See Also == {{Commonscat|Kumomi-Sengen-jinja}} * [[Shrine ranking system]] * {{ill|Mount Eboshi (Matsuzaki, Shizuoka)|ja|烏帽子山 (松崎町)|12=draft}} * {{ill|Mount Ōmuro (Shizuoka)|ja|大室山 (静岡県)|12=draft}} - Another Asama Shrine dedicated solely to Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto == External Links == * [https://www.town.matsuzaki.shizuoka.jp/docs/2020120700015/ Sengen Shrine] - Matsuzaki Town * [http://www.fujisan-jinja.com/shizuoka/kumomi_sengen/ Kumomi Sengen Shrine] - Shrine of Mount Fuji {{Shinto navbox}} {{Iwanaga Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Top level categories]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:refreshing redirects]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Kumomi Sengen Shrine]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Matsuzaki, Shizuoka]] [[Category:History of Matsuzaki, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Asama shrines]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha of Izu Province]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Iwanaga-hime]] [[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[ja:雲見浅間神社]] [[en:Kumomi Sengen Shrine]] [[sn:Kumomi Sengen Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kumomi Sengen Shrine}}

It is traditionally identified with "Ishito no Myojin," listed as "Conferred Fourth Rank, Upper Grade" in the ''Izu Province Shrine Ranking Book,'' and believed to correspond to "Iwanohime no Mikoto Shrine" in the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho,'' although conclusive evidence is lacking. Shrine lore recounts that the main hall was swept into the sea by a typhoon and rebuilt in [[1657]] (Meireki 3). The shrine was a center for [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism) and visited by ascetics until the [[Meiji Restoration]] when it was designated as a [[Gōsha]] following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism. After the abolition of the shrine ranking system post-[[World War II]], local residents have maintained the shrine. == Legends == {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|lt=Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫尊|12=draft}}, the enshrined deity, is the sister of {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花咲耶姫命|12=draft}}, the deity of [[Mount Fuji]] and the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|Fujisan Hongŭ Sengen Taisha]], and daughter of {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山祗命|1=Ōyamatsumi|12=draft}}. In mythology, Iwanaga-hime was considered unattractive but promised longevity like a rock to her offspring, while her beautiful sister Konohanasakuya-hime’s children were destined for short lives like flowers. The heavenly grandson {{ill|Ninigi|ja|瓊瓊杵命|12=draft}} rejected Iwanaga-hime, causing her to retreat to Kumomi, leading to enmity between the once-close sisters. According to {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長|12=draft}}'s ''Kojikiden,'' the jealous Iwanaga-hime fled to Kumomi, where she was enshrined on a small rocky mountain, while her kind sister stretched tall to look for her, becoming the towering Mount Fuji. Local legends say that when Mount Eboshi is clear, Mount Fuji is shrouded in clouds, and vice versa. Praising Mount Fuji from Mount Eboshi is said to invite misfortune, and "Fuji" was once taboo in local speech. == References == * ''Izu Encyclopedia,'' edited by the Izu Studies Association, Hagoromo Publishing, 2010. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == See Also == {{Commonscat|Kumomi-Sengen-jinja}} * [[Shrine ranking system]] * {{ill|Mount Eboshi (Matsuzaki, Shizuoka)|ja|烏帽子山 (松崎町)|12=draft}} * {{ill|Mount Ōmuro (Shizuoka)|ja|大室山 (静岡県)|12=draft}} - Another Asama Shrine dedicated solely to Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto == External Links == * [https://www.town.matsuzaki.shizuoka.jp/docs/2020120700015/ Sengen Shrine] - Matsuzaki Town * [http://www.fujisan-jinja.com/shizuoka/kumomi_sengen/ Kumomi Sengen Shrine] - Shrine of Mount Fuji {{Shinto navbox}} {{Iwanaga Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Top level categories]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Kumomi Sengen Shrine]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Matsuzaki, Shizuoka]] [[Category:History of Matsuzaki, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Asama shrines]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha of Izu Province]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Iwanaga-hime]] [[Category:completion tier 2]] }} [[ja:雲見浅間神社]] [[en:Kumomi Sengen Shrine]] [[sn:Kumomi Sengen Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kumomi Sengen Shrine}}

{{Cite sources|date=2018-08-30}} {{Page number|date=2018-08-30}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name=Kumomi Sengen Shrine | image=[[File:Kumomi-Sengen-jinja (Honden).jpg|250px|Main Hall of Kumomi Sengen Shrine (Photographed on February 11, 2017)]]
Main Hall of Kumomi Sengen Shrine
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|coord={{coord|34|43|31.7|N|138|44|24.5|E}}}} | location=[[Shizuoka Prefecture]][[Kamo District, Shizuoka|Kamo District]][[Matsuzaki, Shizuoka|Matsuzaki Town]] Kumomi 386-2 | Latitude = 34 | Latitude_min = 43 | Latitude_sec = 31.7 | N/S = N | Longitude = 138 | Longitude_min = 44 | Longitude_sec = 24.5 | E/W = E | ISO = JP-22 | Map = Japan Shizuoka | Label Position = left | deity=[[Iwanaga-hime|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto]] | Shrine Rank=[[Gōsha]], (Traditionally) [[Shikinaisha]] | Established=Unknown | Annual Festival=[[July 14]], [[July 15]], [[July 16]] }} '''Kumomi Sengen Shrine''' (雲見浅間神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located on {{ill|Mount Eboshi (Matsuzaki, Shizuoka)|ja|烏帽子山 (松崎町)|12=draft}}, with an elevation of 162 meters, in {{ill|Kumomi Onsen|ja|雲見温泉|12=draft}}, {{ill|Matsuzaki, Shizuoka|ja|松崎町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県|12=draft}}, Japan. It is also known as '''Kumomi Shrine''' (雲見神社) or '''Ontake Sengen Shrine''' (御嶽浅間宮). It is one of the rare [[Sengen shrine|Asama Shrines]] dedicated solely to {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|lt=Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫尊|12=draft}}. == Overview == Mount Eboshi, also called Ontake (御嶽山) or Kumomi-ga-take (雲見ヶ嶽), is a stand-alone {{ill|Tuff|ja|凝灰岩|12=draft}} rock formation located at the westernmost coast of the Izu Peninsula. The shrine's main hall is located just below the summit, while the worship hall and the middle shrine (Nakanomiya) are on the mountain's slopes. Historically, the shrine was off-limits to women, who were only allowed to worship at the middle shrine. From the base at {{ill|National Route 136 (Japan)|ja|国道136号|12=draft}}, the shrine is accessible by a steep 25-minute climb, passing two worship halls before reaching the main shrine. The 428-step stone stairway is extremely steep and includes sections with unstable footing. Due to strong winds near the summit, visitors require sturdy footwear, and light clothing such as high heels is strongly discouraged. It is traditionally identified with "Ishito no Myojin," listed as "Conferred Fourth Rank, Upper Grade" in the ''Izu Province Shrine Ranking Book,'' and believed to correspond to "Iwanohime no Mikoto Shrine" in the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho,'' although conclusive evidence is lacking. The shrine was a center for [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism) and visited by ascetics until the [[Meiji Restoration]] when it was designated as a [[Gōsha]] following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism. After the abolition of the shrine ranking system post-[[World War II]], local residents have maintained the shrine. == Legends == {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|lt=Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫尊|12=draft}}, the enshrined deity, is the sister of {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花咲耶姫命|12=draft}}, the deity of [[Mount Fuji]] and the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|Fujisan Hongŭ Sengen Taisha]], and daughter of {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山祗命|1=Ōyamatsumi|12=draft}}. According to {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長|12=draft}}'s ''Kojikiden,'' the jealous Iwanaga-hime fled to Kumomi, where she was enshrined on a small rocky mountain, while her kind sister stretched tall to look for her, becoming the towering Mount Fuji. Local legends say that when Mount Eboshi is clear, Mount Fuji is shrouded in clouds, and vice versa. Praising Mount Fuji from Mount Eboshi is said to invite misfortune, and "Fuji" was once taboo in local speech. == References == * ''Izu Encyclopedia,'' edited by the Izu Studies Association, Hagoromo Publishing, 2010. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == See Also == {{Commonscat|Kumomi-Sengen-jinja}} * [[Shrine ranking system]] * {{ill|Mount Eboshi (Matsuzaki, Shizuoka)|ja|烏帽子山 (松崎町)|12=draft}} * {{ill|Mount Ōmuro (Shizuoka)|ja|大室山 (静岡県)|12=draft}} - Another Asama Shrine dedicated solely to Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto == External Links == * [https://www.town.matsuzaki.shizuoka.jp/docs/2020120700015/ Sengen Shrine] - Matsuzaki Town * [http://www.fujisan-jinja.com/shizuoka/kumomi_sengen/ Kumomi Sengen Shrine] - Shrine of Mount Fuji {{Shinto navbox}} {{Iwanaga Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Top level categories]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Kumomi Sengen Shrine]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Matsuzaki, Shizuoka]] [[Category:History of Matsuzaki, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Asama shrines]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha of Izu Province]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Iwanaga-hime]] [[Category:completion tier 2]] }} [[ja:雲見浅間神社]] [[en:Kumomi Sengen Shrine]] [[sn:Kumomi Sengen Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kumomi Sengen Shrine}}

Due to strong winds near the summit, visitors require sturdy footwear, and light clothing such as high heels is strongly discouraged. Kumomi-Sengen-jinja (Honden s3).jpg|Honden Kumomi-Sengen-jinja (Haiden s2).jpg|Haiden Kumomi-Sengen-jinja (Nakanomiya).jpg|Nakanomiya Meotomatsu Kumomi-Sengen-jinja.jpg|Sacred [[pine tree]] known as "Meoto Matsu" == History == The founding date and origins are unknown. It is traditionally identified with "Ishito no Myojin," listed as "Conferred Fourth Rank, Upper Grade" in the ''Izu Province Shrine Ranking Book,'' and believed to correspond to "Iwanohime no Mikoto Shrine" in the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho,'' although conclusive evidence is lacking. According to the ''Zushu Shiko,'' the name "Ishito no Myojin" derives from a stone gate called "Asama-mon" in the sea, likely referring to the '''Senganmon''' rock formation about 800 meters south of Mount Eboshi. Shrine lore recounts that the main hall was swept into the sea by a typhoon and rebuilt in [[1657]] (Meireki 3). The shrine was a center for [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism) and visited by ascetics until the [[Meiji Restoration]] when it was designated as a [[Gōsha]] following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism. After the abolition of the shrine ranking system post-[[World War II]], local residents have maintained the shrine. == Legends == {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|lt=Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫尊|12=draft}}, the enshrined deity, is the sister of {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花咲耶姫命|12=draft}}, the deity of [[Mount Fuji]] and the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|Fujisan Hongŭ Sengen Taisha]], and daughter of {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山祗命|1=Ōyamatsumi|12=draft}}. In mythology, Iwanaga-hime was considered unattractive but promised longevity like a rock to her offspring, while her beautiful sister Konohanasakuya-hime’s children were destined for short lives like flowers. The heavenly grandson {{ill|Ninigi|ja|瓊瓊杵命|12=draft}} rejected Iwanaga-hime, causing her to retreat to Kumomi, leading to enmity between the once-close sisters. According to {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長|12=draft}}'s ''Kojikiden,'' the jealous Iwanaga-hime fled to Kumomi, where she was enshrined on a small rocky mountain, while her kind sister stretched tall to look for her, becoming the towering Mount Fuji. Local legends say that when Mount Eboshi is clear, Mount Fuji is shrouded in clouds, and vice versa. Praising Mount Fuji from Mount Eboshi is said to invite misfortune, and "Fuji" was once taboo in local speech. == References == * ''Izu Encyclopedia,'' edited by the Izu Studies Association, Hagoromo Publishing, 2010. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == See Also == {{Commonscat|Kumomi-Sengen-jinja}} * [[Shrine ranking system]] * {{ill|Mount Eboshi (Matsuzaki, Shizuoka)|ja|烏帽子山 (松崎町)|12=draft}} * {{ill|Mount Ōmuro (Shizuoka)|ja|大室山 (静岡県)|12=draft}} - Another Asama Shrine dedicated solely to Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto == External Links == * [https://www.town.matsuzaki.shizuoka.jp/docs/2020120700015/ Sengen Shrine] - Matsuzaki Town * [http://www.fujisan-jinja.com/shizuoka/kumomi_sengen/ Kumomi Sengen Shrine] - Shrine of Mount Fuji {{Shinto navbox}} {{Iwanaga Faith}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Top level categories]] [[Category:refreshing redirects]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Kumomi Sengen Shrine]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Matsuzaki, Shizuoka]] [[Category:History of Matsuzaki, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Asama shrines]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha of Izu Province]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Iwanaga-hime]] }} [[ja:雲見浅間神社]] [[en:Kumomi Sengen Shrine]] [[sn:Kumomi Sengen Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kumomi Sengen Shrine}}

From the base at {{ill|National Route 136 (Japan)|ja|国道136号|12=draft}}, the shrine is accessible by a steep 25-minute climb, passing two worship halls before reaching the main shrine. The 428-step stone stairway is extremely steep and includes sections with unstable footing. Due to strong winds near the summit, visitors require sturdy footwear, and light clothing such as high heels is strongly discouraged. Shrine lore recounts that the main hall was swept into the sea by a typhoon and rebuilt in [[1657]] (Meireki 3). The shrine was a center for [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism) and visited by ascetics until the [[Meiji Restoration]] when it was designated as a [[Gōsha]] following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism. The heavenly grandson {{ill|Ninigi|ja|瓊瓊杵命|12=draft}} rejected Iwanaga-hime, causing her to retreat to Kumomi, leading to enmity between the once-close sisters. It is also known as '''Kumomi Shrine''' (雲見神社) or '''Ontake Sengen Shrine''' (御嶽浅間宮). It is one of the rare [[Sengen shrine|Asama Shrines]] dedicated solely to {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|lt=Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫尊}}. == Overview == Mount Eboshi, also called Ontake (御嶽山) or Kumomi-ga-take (雲見ヶ嶽), is a stand-alone {{ill|Tuff|ja|凝灰岩}} rock formation located at the westernmost coast of the Izu Peninsula. The shrine's main hall is located just below the summit, while the worship hall and the middle shrine (Nakanomiya) are on the mountain's slopes. Historically, the shrine was off-limits to women, who were only allowed to worship at the middle shrine. From the base at {{ill|National Route 136 (Japan)|ja|国道136号}}, the shrine is accessible by a steep 25-minute climb, passing two worship halls before reaching the main shrine. The 428-step stone stairway is extremely steep and includes sections with unstable footing. Due to strong winds near the summit, visitors require sturdy footwear, and light clothing such as high heels is strongly discouraged. Kumomi-Sengen-jinja (Honden s3).jpg|Honden Kumomi-Sengen-jinja (Haiden s2).jpg|Haiden Kumomi-Sengen-jinja (Nakanomiya).jpg|Nakanomiya Meotomatsu Kumomi-Sengen-jinja.jpg|Sacred [[pine tree]] known as "Meoto Matsu" == History == The founding date and origins are unknown. It is traditionally identified with "Ishito no Myojin," listed as "Conferred Fourth Rank, Upper Grade" in the ''Izu Province Shrine Ranking Book,'' and believed to correspond to "Iwanohime no Mikoto Shrine" in the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho,'' although conclusive evidence is lacking. According to the ''Zushu Shiko,'' the name "Ishito no Myojin" derives from a stone gate called "Asama-mon" in the sea, likely referring to the '''Senganmon''' rock formation about 800 meters south of Mount Eboshi. Shrine lore recounts that the main hall was swept into the sea by a typhoon and rebuilt in [[1657]] (Meireki 3). The shrine was a center for [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism) and visited by ascetics until the [[Meiji Restoration]] when it was designated as a [[Gōsha]] following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism. After the abolition of the shrine Ranking system post-[[World War II]], local residents have maintained the shrine. == Legends == {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|lt=Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫尊}}, the enshrined deity, is the sister of {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花咲耶姫命}}, the deity of [[Mount Fuji]] and the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|Fujisan Hongŭ Sengen Taisha]], and daughter of {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山祗命|1=Ōyamatsumi}}. In mythology, Iwanaga-hime was considered unattractive but promised longevity like a rock to her offspring, while her beautiful sister Konohanasakuya-hime’s children were destined for short lives like flowers. The heavenly grandson {{ill|Ninigi|ja|瓊瓊杵命}} rejected Iwanaga-hime, causing her to retreat to Kumomi, leading to enmity between the once-close sisters. According to {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}}'s ''Kojikiden,'' the jealous Iwanaga-hime fled to Kumomi, where she was enshrined on a small rocky mountain, while her kind sister stretched tall to look for her, becoming the towering Mount Fuji. Local legends say that when Mount Eboshi is clear, Mount Fuji is shrouded in clouds, and vice versa. Praising Mount Fuji from Mount Eboshi is said to invite misfortune, and "Fuji" was once taboo in local speech. == References == * ''Izu Encyclopedia,'' edited by the Izu Studies Association, Hagoromo Publishing, 2010. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == See Also == {{Commonscat|Kumomi-Sengen-jinja}} * [[Shrine Ranking system]] * {{ill|Mount Eboshi (Matsuzaki, Shizuoka)|ja|烏帽子山 (松崎町)}} * {{ill|Mount Ōmuro (Shizuoka)|ja|大室山 (静岡県)}} - Another Asama Shrine dedicated solely to Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto == External Links == * [https://www.town.matsuzaki.shizuoka.jp/docs/2020120700015/ Sengen Shrine] - Matsuzaki Town * [http://www.fujisan-jinja.com/shizuoka/kumomi_sengen/ Kumomi Sengen Shrine] - Shrine of Mount Fuji {{Shinto navbox}} {{Iwanaga Faith}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Top level categories]] [[Category:refreshing redirects]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Kumomi Sengen Shrine]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Matsuzaki, Shizuoka]] [[Category:History of Matsuzaki, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Asama shrines]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha of Izu Province]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Iwanaga-hime]] }} [[ja:雲見浅間神社]] [[en:Kumomi Sengen Shrine]] [[sn:Kumomi Sengen Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kumomi Sengen Shrine}} [[Category:Ambox transclusions]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kumomi Sengen Shrine]][[Category:qq]]

After the abolition of the shrine Ranking system post-[[World War II]], local residents have maintained the shrine. == Legends == {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|lt=Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫尊}}, the enshrined deity, is the sister of {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花咲耶姫命}}, the deity of [[Mount Fuji]] and the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|Fujisan Hongŭ Sengen Taisha]], and daughter of {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山祗命|1=Ōyamatsumi}}. The heavenly grandson {{ill|Ninigi|ja|瓊瓊杵命}} rejected Iwanaga-hime, causing her to retreat to Kumomi, leading to enmity between the once-close sisters. Praising Mount Fuji from Mount Eboshi is said to invite misfortune, and "Fuji" was once taboo in local speech. == References == * ''Izu Encyclopedia,'' edited by the Izu Studies Association, Hagoromo Publishing, 2010. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == See Also == {{Commonscat|Kumomi-Sengen-jinja}} * [[Shrine Ranking system]] * {{ill|Mount Eboshi (Matsuzaki, Shizuoka)|ja|烏帽子山 (松崎町)|12=draft}} * {{ill|Mount Ōmuro (Shizuoka)|ja|大室山 (静岡県)|12=draft}} - Another Asama Shrine dedicated solely to Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto == External Links == * [https://www.town.matsuzaki.shizuoka.jp/docs/2020120700015/ Sengen Shrine] - Matsuzaki Town * [http://www.fujisan-jinja.com/shizuoka/kumomi_sengen/ Kumomi Sengen Shrine] - Shrine of Mount Fuji {{Shinto navbox}} {{Iwanaga Faith}} {{translated page|ja|雲見浅間神社|version=103489865|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kumomi Sengen Shrine}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Top level categories]] [[Category:refreshing redirects]] [[Category:Kumomi Sengen Shrine]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Matsuzaki, Shizuoka]] [[Category:History of Matsuzaki, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Asama shrines]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha of Izu Province]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Iwanaga-hime]] [[Category:Ambox transclusions]] }} [[ja:雲見浅間神社]] [[en:Kumomi Sengen Shrine]] [[sn:Kumomi Sengen Shrine]]

{{Cite sources|date=2018-08-30}} {{Page number|date=2018-08-30}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name=Kumomi Sengen Shrine | image=[[File:Kumomi-Sengen-jinja (Honden).jpg|250px|Main Hall of Kumomi Sengen Shrine (Photographed on February 11, 2017)]]
Main Hall of Kumomi Sengen Shrine
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|coord={{coord|34|43|31.7|N|138|44|24.5|E}}}} | location=[[Shizuoka Prefecture]][[Kamo District, Shizuoka|Kamo District]][[Matsuzaki, Shizuoka|Matsuzaki Town]] Kumomi 386-2 | Latitude = 34 | Latitude_min = 43 | Latitude_sec = 31.7 | N/S = N | Longitude = 138 | Longitude_min = 44 | Longitude_sec = 24.5 | E/W = E | ISO = JP-22 | Map = Japan Shizuoka | Label Position = left | deity=[[Iwanaga-hime|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto]] | Shrine Rank=[[Gōsha]], (Traditionally) [[Shikinaisha]] | Established=Unknown | Annual Festival=[[July 14]], [[July 15]], [[July 16]] }} '''Kumomi Sengen Shrine''' (雲見浅間神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located on {{ill|Mount Eboshi (Matsuzaki, Shizuoka)|ja|烏帽子山 (松崎町)|qq=draft}}, with an elevation of 162 meters, in {{ill|Kumomi Onsen|ja|雲見温泉|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Matsuzaki, Shizuoka|ja|松崎町|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県|qq=draft}}, Japan. It is also known as '''Kumomi Shrine''' (雲見神社) or '''Ontake Sengen Shrine''' (御嶽浅間宮). It is one of the rare [[Sengen shrine|Asama Shrines]] dedicated solely to {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|lt=Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫尊|qq=draft}}. == Overview == Mount Eboshi, also called Ontake (御嶽山) or Kumomi-ga-take (雲見ヶ嶽), is a stand-alone {{ill|Tuff|ja|凝灰岩|qq=draft}} rock formation located at the westernmost coast of the Izu Peninsula. The shrine's main hall is located just below the summit, while the worship hall and the middle shrine (Nakanomiya) are on the mountain's slopes. Historically, the shrine was off-limits to women, who were only allowed to worship at the middle shrine. From the base at {{ill|National Route 136 (Japan)|ja|国道136号|qq=draft}}, the shrine is accessible by a steep 25-minute climb, passing two worship halls before reaching the main shrine. The 428-step stone stairway is extremely steep and includes sections with unstable footing. Due to strong winds near the summit, visitors require sturdy footwear, and light clothing such as high heels is strongly discouraged. Kumomi-Sengen-jinja (Honden s3).jpg|Honden Kumomi-Sengen-jinja (Haiden s2).jpg|Haiden Kumomi-Sengen-jinja (Nakanomiya).jpg|Nakanomiya Meotomatsu Kumomi-Sengen-jinja.jpg|Sacred [[pine tree]] known as "Meoto Matsu" == History == The founding date and origins are unknown. It is traditionally identified with "Ishito no Myojin," listed as "Conferred Fourth Rank, Upper Grade" in the ''Izu Province Shrine Ranking Book,'' and believed to correspond to "Iwanohime no Mikoto Shrine" in the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho,'' although conclusive evidence is lacking. According to the ''Zushu Shiko,'' the name "Ishito no Myojin" derives from a stone gate called "Asama-mon" in the sea, likely referring to the '''Senganmon''' rock formation about 800 meters south of Mount Eboshi. Shrine lore recounts that the main hall was swept into the sea by a typhoon and rebuilt in [[1657]] (Meireki 3). The shrine was a center for [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism) and visited by ascetics until the [[Meiji Restoration]] when it was designated as a [[Gōsha]] following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism. After the abolition of the shrine Ranking system post-[[World War II]], local residents have maintained the shrine. == Legends == {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|lt=Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫尊|qq=draft}}, the enshrined deity, is the sister of {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花咲耶姫命|qq=draft}}, the deity of [[Mount Fuji]] and the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|Fujisan Hongŭ Sengen Taisha]], and daughter of {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山祗命|1=Ōyamatsumi|qq=draft}}. In mythology, Iwanaga-hime was considered unattractive but promised longevity like a rock to her offspring, while her beautiful sister Konohanasakuya-hime’s children were destined for short lives like flowers. The heavenly grandson {{ill|Ninigi|ja|瓊瓊杵命|qq=draft}} rejected Iwanaga-hime, causing her to retreat to Kumomi, leading to enmity between the once-close sisters. According to {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長|qq=draft}}'s ''Kojikiden,'' the jealous Iwanaga-hime fled to Kumomi, where she was enshrined on a small rocky mountain, while her kind sister stretched tall to look for her, becoming the towering Mount Fuji. Local legends say that when Mount Eboshi is clear, Mount Fuji is shrouded in clouds, and vice versa. Praising Mount Fuji from Mount Eboshi is said to invite misfortune, and "Fuji" was once taboo in local speech. == References == * ''Izu Encyclopedia,'' edited by the Izu Studies Association, Hagoromo Publishing, 2010. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == See Also == {{Commonscat|Kumomi-Sengen-jinja}} * [[Shrine Ranking system]] * {{ill|Mount Eboshi (Matsuzaki, Shizuoka)|ja|烏帽子山 (松崎町)|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Mount Ōmuro (Shizuoka)|ja|大室山 (静岡県)|qq=draft}} - Another Asama Shrine dedicated solely to Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto == External Links == * [https://www.town.matsuzaki.shizuoka.jp/docs/2020120700015/ Sengen Shrine] - Matsuzaki Town * [http://www.fujisan-jinja.com/shizuoka/kumomi_sengen/ Kumomi Sengen Shrine] - Shrine of Mount Fuji {{Shinto navbox}} {{Iwanaga Faith}} {{translated page|ja|雲見浅間神社|version=103489865|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kumomi Sengen Shrine}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Top level categories]] [[Category:refreshing redirects]] [[Category:Kumomi Sengen Shrine]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Matsuzaki, Shizuoka]] [[Category:History of Matsuzaki, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Asama shrines]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha of Izu Province]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Iwanaga-hime]] [[Category:Ambox transclusions]] }} [[ja:雲見浅間神社]] [[en:Kumomi Sengen Shrine]] [[sn:Kumomi Sengen Shrine]]

In mythology, Iwanaga-hime was considered unattractive but promised longevity like a rock to her offspring, while her beautiful sister Konohanasakuya-hime’s children were destined for short lives like flowers. The heavenly grandson {{ill|Ninigi|ja|瓊瓊杵命|qq=draft}} rejected Iwanaga-hime, causing her to retreat to Kumomi, leading to enmity between the once-close sisters. According to {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長|qq=draft}}'s ''Kojikiden,'' the jealous Iwanaga-hime fled to Kumomi, where she was enshrined on a small rocky mountain, while her kind sister stretched tall to look for her, becoming the towering Mount Fuji. Local legends say that when Mount Eboshi is clear, Mount Fuji is shrouded in clouds, and vice versa. Praising Mount Fuji from Mount Eboshi is said to invite misfortune, and "Fuji" was once taboo in local speech. == References == * ''Izu Encyclopedia,'' edited by the Izu Studies Association, Hagoromo Publishing, 2010. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == See Also == {{Commonscat|Kumomi-Sengen-jinja}} * [[Shrine Ranking system]] * {{ill|Mount Eboshi (Matsuzaki, Shizuoka)|ja|烏帽子山 (松崎町)|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Mount Ōmuro (Shizuoka)|ja|大室山 (静岡県)|qq=draft}} - Another Asama Shrine dedicated solely to Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto == External Links == * [https://www.town.matsuzaki.shizuoka.jp/docs/2020120700015/ Sengen Shrine] - Matsuzaki Town * [http://www.fujisan-jinja.com/shizuoka/kumomi_sengen/ Kumomi Sengen Shrine] - Shrine of Mount Fuji {{Shinto navbox}} {{Iwanaga Faith}} {{translated page|ja|雲見浅間神社|version=103489865|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Top level categories]] [[Category:refreshing redirects]] [[Category:Kumomi Sengen Shrine]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Matsuzaki, Shizuoka]] [[Category:History of Matsuzaki, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Asama shrines]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Iwanaga-hime]] }} [[ja:雲見浅間神社]] [[en:Kumomi Sengen Shrine]] [[sn:Kumomi Sengen Shrine]]

{{Cite sources|date=2018-08-30}} {{Page number|date=2018-08-30}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name=Kumomi Sengen Shrine | image=[[File:Kumomi-Sengen-jinja (Honden).jpg|250px|Main Hall of Kumomi Sengen Shrine (Photographed on February 11, 2017)]]
Main Hall of Kumomi Sengen Shrine
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|coord={{coord|34|43|31.7|N|138|44|24.5|E}}}} | location=[[Shizuoka Prefecture]][[Kamo District, Shizuoka|Kamo District]][[Matsuzaki, Shizuoka|Matsuzaki Town]] Kumomi 386-2 | Latitude = 34 | Latitude_min = 43 | Latitude_sec = 31.7 | N/S = N | Longitude = 138 | Longitude_min = 44 | Longitude_sec = 24.5 | E/W = E | ISO = JP-22 | Map = Japan Shizuoka | Label Position = left | deity=[[Iwanaga-hime|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto]] | Shrine Rank=[[Gōsha]], (Traditionally) [[Shikinaisha]] | Established=Unknown | Annual Festival=[[July 14]], [[July 15]], [[July 16]] }} '''Kumomi Sengen Shrine''' (雲見浅間神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located on {{ill|Mount Eboshi (Matsuzaki, Shizuoka)|ja|烏帽子山 (松崎町)|qq=draft}}, with an elevation of 162 meters, in {{ill|Kumomi Onsen|ja|雲見温泉|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Matsuzaki, Shizuoka|ja|松崎町|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県|qq=draft}}, Japan. It is also known as '''Kumomi Shrine''' (雲見神社) or '''Ontake Sengen Shrine''' (御嶽浅間宮). It is one of the rare [[Sengen shrine|Asama Shrines]] dedicated solely to {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|lt=Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫尊|qq=draft}}. == Overview == Mount Eboshi, also called Ontake (御嶽山) or Kumomi-ga-take (雲見ヶ嶽), is a stand-alone {{ill|Tuff|ja|凝灰岩|qq=draft}} rock formation located at the westernmost coast of the Izu Peninsula. The shrine's main hall is located just below the summit, while the worship hall and the middle shrine (Nakanomiya) are on the mountain's slopes. Historically, the shrine was off-limits to women, who were only allowed to worship at the middle shrine. From the base at {{ill|National Route 136 (Japan)|ja|国道136号|qq=draft}}, the shrine is accessible by a steep 25-minute climb, passing two worship halls before reaching the main shrine. The 428-step stone stairway is extremely steep and includes sections with unstable footing. Due to strong winds near the summit, visitors require sturdy footwear, and light clothing such as high heels is strongly discouraged. Kumomi-Sengen-jinja (Honden s3).jpg|Honden Kumomi-Sengen-jinja (Haiden s2).jpg|Haiden Kumomi-Sengen-jinja (Nakanomiya).jpg|Nakanomiya Meotomatsu Kumomi-Sengen-jinja.jpg|Sacred [[pine tree]] known as "Meoto Matsu" == History == The founding date and origins are unknown. It is traditionally identified with "Ishito no Myojin," listed as "Conferred Fourth Rank, Upper Grade" in the ''Izu Province Shrine Ranking Book,'' and believed to correspond to "Iwanohime no Mikoto Shrine" in the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho,'' although conclusive evidence is lacking. According to the ''Zushu Shiko,'' the name "Ishito no Myojin" derives from a stone gate called "Asama-mon" in the sea, likely referring to the '''Senganmon''' rock formation about 800 meters south of Mount Eboshi. Shrine lore recounts that the main hall was swept into the sea by a typhoon and rebuilt in [[1657]] (Meireki 3). The shrine was a center for [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism) and visited by ascetics until the [[Meiji Restoration]] when it was designated as a [[Gōsha]] following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism. After the abolition of the shrine Ranking system post-[[World War II]], local residents have maintained the shrine. == Legends == {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|lt=Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫尊|qq=draft}}, the enshrined deity, is the sister of {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花咲耶姫命|qq=draft}}, the deity of [[Mount Fuji]] and the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|Fujisan Hongŭ Sengen Taisha]], and daughter of {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山祗命|1=Ōyamatsumi|qq=draft}}. In mythology, Iwanaga-hime was considered unattractive but promised longevity like a rock to her offspring, while her beautiful sister Konohanasakuya-hime’s children were destined for short lives like flowers. The heavenly grandson {{ill|Ninigi|ja|瓊瓊杵命|qq=draft}} rejected Iwanaga-hime, causing her to retreat to Kumomi, leading to enmity between the once-close sisters. According to {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長|qq=draft}}'s ''Kojikiden,'' the jealous Iwanaga-hime fled to Kumomi, where she was enshrined on a small rocky mountain, while her kind sister stretched tall to look for her, becoming the towering Mount Fuji. Local legends say that when Mount Eboshi is clear, Mount Fuji is shrouded in clouds, and vice versa. Praising Mount Fuji from Mount Eboshi is said to invite misfortune, and "Fuji" was once taboo in local speech. == References == * ''Izu Encyclopedia,'' edited by the Izu Studies Association, Hagoromo Publishing, 2010. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == See Also == {{Commonscat|Kumomi-Sengen-jinja}} * [[Shrine Ranking system]] * {{ill|Mount Eboshi (Matsuzaki, Shizuoka)|ja|烏帽子山 (松崎町)|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Mount Ōmuro (Shizuoka)|ja|大室山 (静岡県)|qq=draft}} - Another Asama Shrine dedicated solely to Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto == External Links == * [https://www.town.matsuzaki.shizuoka.jp/docs/2020120700015/ Sengen Shrine] - Matsuzaki Town * [http://www.fujisan-jinja.com/shizuoka/kumomi_sengen/ Kumomi Sengen Shrine] - Shrine of Mount Fuji {{Shinto navbox}} {{Iwanaga Faith}} {{translated page|ja|雲見浅間神社|version=103489865|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Top level categories]] [[Category:refreshing redirects]] [[Category:Kumomi Sengen Shrine]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Matsuzaki, Shizuoka]] [[Category:History of Matsuzaki, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Asama shrines]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Iwanaga-hime]] }} [[ja:雲見浅間神社]] [[en:Kumomi Sengen Shrine]] [[sn:Kumomi Sengen Shrine]]

It is traditionally identified with "Ishito no Myojin," listed as "Conferred Fourth Rank, Upper Grade" in the ''Izu Province Shrine Ranking Book,'' and believed to correspond to "Iwanohime no Mikoto Shrine" in the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho,'' although conclusive evidence is lacking. According to the ''Zushu Shiko,'' the name "Ishito no Myojin" derives from a stone gate called "Asama-mon" in the sea, likely referring to the '''Senganmon''' rock formation about 800 meters south of Mount Eboshi. Shrine lore recounts that the main hall was swept into the sea by a typhoon and rebuilt in [[1657]] (Meireki 3). The shrine was a center for [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism) and visited by ascetics until the [[Meiji Restoration]] when it was designated as a [[Gōsha]] following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism. After the abolition of the shrine Ranking system post-[[World War II]], local residents have maintained the shrine. == Legends == {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|lt=Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫尊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanaga-hime|qq=}}, the enshrined deity, is the sister of {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花咲耶姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|qq=}}, the deity of [[Mount Fuji]] and the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|Fujisan Hongŭ Sengen Taisha]], and daughter of {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山祗命|1=Ōyamatsumi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi|qq=}}. In mythology, Iwanaga-hime was considered unattractive but promised longevity like a rock to her offspring, while her beautiful sister Konohanasakuya-hime’s children were destined for short lives like flowers. The heavenly grandson {{ill|Ninigi|ja|瓊瓊杵命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninigi|qq=}} rejected Iwanaga-hime, causing her to retreat to Kumomi, leading to enmity between the once-close sisters. According to {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Motoori Norinaga|qq=}}'s ''Kojikiden,'' the jealous Iwanaga-hime fled to Kumomi, where she was enshrined on a small rocky mountain, while her kind sister stretched tall to look for her, becoming the towering Mount Fuji. Local legends say that when Mount Eboshi is clear, Mount Fuji is shrouded in clouds, and vice versa. {{Unreferenceds|date=2018-08-30}} {{Page number|date=2018-08-30}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name=Kumomi Sengen Shrine | image=[[File:Kumomi-Sengen-jinja (Honden).jpg|250px|Main Hall of Kumomi Sengen Shrine (Photographed on February 11, 2017)]]
Main Hall of Kumomi Sengen Shrine
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|coord={{coord|34|43|31.7|N|138|44|24.5|E}}}} | location=[[Shizuoka Prefecture]][[Kamo District, Shizuoka|Kamo District]][[Matsuzaki, Shizuoka|Matsuzaki Town]] Kumomi 386-2 | Latitude = 34 | Latitude_min = 43 | Latitude_sec = 31.7 | N/S = N | Longitude = 138 | Longitude_min = 44 | Longitude_sec = 24.5 | E/W = E | ISO = JP-22 | Map = Japan Shizuoka | Label Position = left | deity=[[Iwanaga-hime|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto]] | Shrine Rank=[[Gōsha]], (Traditionally) [[Shikinaisha]] | Established=Unknown | Annual Festival=[[July 14]], [[July 15]], [[July 16]] }} '''Kumomi Sengen Shrine''' (雲見浅間神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located on {{ill|Mount Eboshi (Matsuzaki, Shizuoka)|ja|烏帽子山 (松崎町)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Eboshi (Matsuzaki, Shizuoka)|qq=}}, with an elevation of 162 meters, in {{ill|Kumomi Onsen|ja|雲見温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumomi Onsen|qq=}}, {{ill|Matsuzaki, Shizuoka|ja|松崎町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsuzaki, Shizuoka|qq=}}, {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shizuoka Prefecture|qq=}}, Japan. It is also known as '''Kumomi Shrine''' (雲見神社) or '''Ontake Sengen Shrine''' (御嶽浅間宮). It is one of the rare [[Sengen shrine|Asama Shrines]] dedicated solely to {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|lt=Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫尊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanaga-hime|qq=}}. == Overview == Mount Eboshi, also called Ontake (御嶽山) or Kumomi-ga-take (雲見ヶ嶽), is a stand-alone {{ill|Tuff|ja|凝灰岩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tuff|qq=}} rock formation located at the westernmost coast of the Izu Peninsula. The shrine's main hall is located just below the summit, while the worship hall and the middle shrine (Nakanomiya) are on the mountain's slopes. Historically, the shrine was off-limits to women, who were only allowed to worship at the middle shrine. From the base at {{ill|National Route 136 (Japan)|ja|国道136号|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Route 136 (Japan)|qq=}}, the shrine is accessible by a steep 25-minute climb, passing two worship halls before reaching the main shrine. The 428-step stone stairway is extremely steep and includes sections with unstable footing. Due to strong winds near the summit, visitors require sturdy footwear, and light clothing such as high heels is strongly discouraged. Praising Mount Fuji from Mount Eboshi is said to invite misfortune, and "Fuji" was once taboo in local speech. == References == * ''Izu Encyclopedia,'' edited by the Izu Studies Association, Hagoromo Publishing, 2010. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == See Also == {{Commonscat|Kumomi-Sengen-jinja}} * [[Shrine Ranking system]] * {{ill|Mount Eboshi (Matsuzaki, Shizuoka)|ja|烏帽子山 (松崎町)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Eboshi (Matsuzaki, Shizuoka)|qq=}} * {{ill|Mount Ōmuro (Shizuoka)|ja|大室山 (静岡県)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Ōmuro (Shizuoka)|qq=}} - Another Asama Shrine dedicated solely to Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto == External Links == * [https://www.town.matsuzaki.shizuoka.jp/docs/2020120700015/ Sengen Shrine] - Matsuzaki Town * [http://www.fujisan-jinja.com/shizuoka/kumomi_sengen/ Kumomi Sengen Shrine] - Shrine of Mount Fuji {{Shinto navbox}} {{Iwanaga Faith}} {{translated page|ja|雲見浅間神社|version=103489865|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Top level categories]] [[Category:Kumomi Sengen Shrine]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Matsuzaki, Shizuoka]] [[Category:History of Matsuzaki, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Asama shrines]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Iwanaga-hime]] }} [[ja:雲見浅間神社]] [[en:Kumomi Sengen Shrine]] [[sn:Kumomi Sengen Shrine]]

revision.text:
{{Unreferenced|date=2018-08-30}} {{Page number|date=2018-08-30}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name=Kumomi Sengen Shrine | image=[[File:Kumomi-Sengen-jinja (Honden).jpg|250px|Main Hall of Kumomi Sengen Shrine (Photographed on February 11, 2017)]]
Main Hall of Kumomi Sengen Shrine
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|coord={{coord|34|43|31.7|N|138|44|24.5|E}}}} | location=[[Shizuoka Prefecture]][[Kamo District, Shizuoka|Kamo District]][[Matsuzaki, Shizuoka|Matsuzaki Town]] Kumomi 386-2 | Latitude = 34 | Latitude_min = 43 | Latitude_sec = 31.7 | N/S = N | Longitude = 138 | Longitude_min = 44 | Longitude_sec = 24.5 | E/W = E | ISO = JP-22 | Map = Japan Shizuoka | Label Position = left | deity=[[Iwanaga-hime|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto]] | Shrine Rank=[[Gōsha]], (Traditionally) [[Shikinaisha]] | Established=Unknown | Annual Festival=[[July 14]], [[July 15]], [[July 16]] }} '''Kumomi Sengen Shrine''' (雲見浅間神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located on {{ill|Mount Eboshi (Matsuzaki, Shizuoka)|ja|烏帽子山 (松崎町)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Eboshi (Matsuzaki, Shizuoka)|qq=}}, with an elevation of 162 meters, in {{ill|Kumomi Onsen|ja|雲見温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumomi Onsen|qq=}}, {{ill|Matsuzaki, Shizuoka|ja|松崎町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsuzaki, Shizuoka|qq=}}, {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shizuoka Prefecture|qq=}}, Japan. It is also known as '''Kumomi Shrine''' (雲見神社) or '''Ontake Sengen Shrine''' (御嶽浅間宮). It is one of the rare [[Sengen shrine|Asama Shrines]] dedicated solely to {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|lt=Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫尊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanaga-hime|qq=}}. == Overview == Mount Eboshi, also called Ontake (御嶽山) or Kumomi-ga-take (雲見ヶ嶽), is a stand-alone {{ill|Tuff|ja|凝灰岩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tuff|qq=}} rock formation located at the westernmost coast of the Izu Peninsula. The shrine's main hall is located just below the summit, while the worship hall and the middle shrine (Nakanomiya) are on the mountain's slopes. Historically, the shrine was off-limits to women, who were only allowed to worship at the middle shrine. From the base at {{ill|National Route 136 (Japan)|ja|国道136号|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Route 136 (Japan)|qq=}}, the shrine is accessible by a steep 25-minute climb, passing two worship halls before reaching the main shrine. The 428-step stone stairway is extremely steep and includes sections with unstable footing. Due to strong winds near the summit, visitors require sturdy footwear, and light clothing such as high heels is strongly discouraged. Kumomi-Sengen-jinja (Honden s3).jpg|Honden Kumomi-Sengen-jinja (Haiden s2).jpg|Haiden Kumomi-Sengen-jinja (Nakanomiya).jpg|Nakanomiya Meotomatsu Kumomi-Sengen-jinja.jpg|Sacred [[pine tree]] known as "Meoto Matsu" == History == The founding date and origins are unknown. It is traditionally identified with "Ishito no Myojin," listed as "Conferred Fourth Rank, Upper Grade" in the ''Izu Province Shrine Ranking Book,'' and believed to correspond to "Iwanohime no Mikoto Shrine" in the ''Engishiki Jinmyōchō,'' although conclusive evidence is lacking. According to the ''Zushu Shiko,'' the name "Ishito no Myojin" derives from a stone gate called "Asama-mon" in the sea, likely referring to the '''Senganmon''' rock formation about 800 meters south of Mount Eboshi. Shrine lore recounts that the main hall was swept into the sea by a typhoon and rebuilt in [[1657]] (Meireki 3). The shrine was a center for [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism) and visited by ascetics until the [[Meiji Restoration]] when it was designated as a [[Gōsha]] following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism. After the abolition of the shrine Ranking system post-[[World War II]], local residents have maintained the shrine. == Legends == {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|lt=Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫尊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanaga-hime|qq=}}, the enshrined deity, is the sister of {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花咲耶姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|qq=}}, the deity of [[Mount Fuji]] and the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|Fujisan Hongŭ Sengen Taisha]], and daughter of {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山祗命|1=Ōyamatsumi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi|qq=}}. In mythology, Iwanaga-hime was considered unattractive but promised longevity like a rock to her offspring, while her beautiful sister Konohanasakuya-hime’s children were destined for short lives like flowers. The heavenly grandson {{ill|Ninigi|ja|瓊瓊杵命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninigi|qq=}} rejected Iwanaga-hime, causing her to retreat to Kumomi, leading to enmity between the once-close sisters. According to {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Motoori Norinaga|qq=}}'s ''Kojikiden,'' the jealous Iwanaga-hime fled to Kumomi, where she was enshrined on a small rocky mountain, while her kind sister stretched tall to look for her, becoming the towering Mount Fuji. Local legends say that when Mount Eboshi is clear, Mount Fuji is shrouded in clouds, and vice versa. Praising Mount Fuji from Mount Eboshi is said to invite misfortune, and "Fuji" was once taboo in local speech. == References == * ''Izu Encyclopedia,'' edited by the Izu Studies Association, Hagoromo Publishing, 2010. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == See Also == {{Commonscat|Kumomi-Sengen-jinja}} * [[Shrine Ranking system]] * {{ill|Mount Eboshi (Matsuzaki, Shizuoka)|ja|烏帽子山 (松崎町)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Eboshi (Matsuzaki, Shizuoka)|qq=}} * {{ill|Mount Ōmuro (Shizuoka)|ja|大室山 (静岡県)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Ōmuro (Shizuoka)|qq=}} - Another Asama Shrine dedicated solely to Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto == External Links == * [https://www.town.matsuzaki.shizuoka.jp/docs/2020120700015/ Sengen Shrine] - Matsuzaki Town * [http://www.fujisan-jinja.com/shizuoka/kumomi_sengen/ Kumomi Sengen Shrine] - Shrine of Mount Fuji {{Shinto navbox}} {{Iwanaga Faith}} {{translated page|ja|雲見浅間神社|version=103489865|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Top level categories]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Matsuzaki, Shizuoka]] [[Category:History of Matsuzaki, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Asama shrines]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Iwanaga-hime]] }} [[ja:雲見浅間神社]] [[en:Kumomi Sengen Shrine]] [[sn:Kumomi Sengen Shrine]]

The 428-step stone stairway is extremely steep and includes sections with unstable footing. Due to strong winds near the summit, visitors require sturdy footwear, and light clothing such as high heels is strongly discouraged. Kumomi-Sengen-jinja (Honden s3).jpg|Honden Kumomi-Sengen-jinja (Haiden s2).jpg|Haiden Kumomi-Sengen-jinja (Nakanomiya).jpg|Nakanomiya Meotomatsu Kumomi-Sengen-jinja.jpg|Sacred [[pine tree]] known as "Meoto Matsu" == History == The founding date and origins are unknown. {{Unreferenced|date=2018-08-30}} {{Page number|date=2018-08-30}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name=Kumomi Sengen Shrine | image=[[File:Kumomi-Sengen-jinja (Honden).jpg|250px|Main Hall of Kumomi Sengen Shrine (Photographed on February 11, 2017)]]
Main Hall of Kumomi Sengen Shrine
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|coord={{coord|34|43|31.7|N|138|44|24.5|E}}}} | location=[[Shizuoka Prefecture]][[Kamo District, Shizuoka|Kamo District]][[Matsuzaki, Shizuoka|Matsuzaki Town]] Kumomi 386-2 | Latitude = 34 | Latitude_min = 43 | Latitude_sec = 31.7 | N/S = N | Longitude = 138 | Longitude_min = 44 | Longitude_sec = 24.5 | E/W = E | ISO = JP-22 | Map = Japan Shizuoka | Label Position = left | deity=[[Iwanaga-hime|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto]] | Shrine Rank=[[Gōsha]], (Traditionally) [[Shikinaisha]] | Established=Unknown | Annual Festival=[[July 14]], [[July 15]], [[July 16]] }} '''Kumomi Sengen Shrine''' (雲見浅間神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located on {{ill|Mount Eboshi (Matsuzaki, Shizuoka)|ja|烏帽子山 (松崎町)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Eboshi (Matsuzaki, Shizuoka)|qq=}}, with an elevation of 162 meters, in {{ill|Kumomi Onsen|ja|雲見温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumomi Onsen|qq=}}, [[:en:Matsuzaki, Shizuoka|Matsuzaki, Shizuoka]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan. It is also known as '''Kumomi Shrine''' (雲見神社) or '''Ontake Sengen Shrine''' (御嶽浅間宮). It is one of the rare [[Sengen shrine|Asama Shrines]] dedicated solely to {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|lt=Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫尊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanaga-hime|qq=}}. == Overview == Mount Eboshi, also called Ontake (御嶽山) or Kumomi-ga-take (雲見ヶ嶽), is a stand-alone [[:en:Tuff|Tuff]] rock formation located at the westernmost coast of the Izu Peninsula. The shrine's main hall is located just below the summit, while the worship hall and the middle shrine (Nakanomiya) are on the mountain's slopes. Historically, the shrine was off-limits to women, who were only allowed to worship at the middle shrine. From the base at [[:en:Japan National Route 136|National Route 136 (Japan)]], the shrine is accessible by a steep 25-minute climb, passing two worship halls before reaching the main shrine. The 428-step stone stairway is extremely steep and includes sections with unstable footing. Due to strong winds near the summit, visitors require sturdy footwear, and light clothing such as high heels is strongly discouraged. Kumomi-Sengen-jinja (Honden s3).jpg|Honden Kumomi-Sengen-jinja (Haiden s2).jpg|Haiden Kumomi-Sengen-jinja (Nakanomiya).jpg|Nakanomiya Meotomatsu Kumomi-Sengen-jinja.jpg|Sacred [[pine tree]] known as "Meoto Matsu" == History == The founding date and origins are unknown. It is traditionally identified with "Ishito no Myojin," listed as "Conferred Fourth Rank, Upper Grade" in the ''Izu Province Shrine Ranking Book,'' and believed to correspond to "Iwanohime no Mikoto Shrine" in the ''Engishiki Jinmyōchō,'' although conclusive evidence is lacking. According to the ''Zushu Shiko,'' the name "Ishito no Myojin" derives from a stone gate called "Asama-mon" in the sea, likely referring to the '''Senganmon''' rock formation about 800 meters south of Mount Eboshi. Shrine lore recounts that the main hall was swept into the sea by a typhoon and rebuilt in [[1657]] (Meireki 3). The shrine was a center for [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism) and visited by ascetics until the [[Meiji Restoration]] when it was designated as a [[Gōsha]] following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism. After the abolition of the shrine Ranking system post-[[World War II]], local residents have maintained the shrine. == Legends == {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|lt=Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫尊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanaga-hime|qq=}}, the enshrined deity, is the sister of {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花咲耶姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|qq=}}, the deity of [[Mount Fuji]] and the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|Fujisan Hongŭ Sengen Taisha]], and daughter of {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山祗命|1=Ōyamatsumi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi|qq=}}. In mythology, Iwanaga-hime was considered unattractive but promised longevity like a rock to her offspring, while her beautiful sister Konohanasakuya-hime’s children were destined for short lives like flowers. The heavenly grandson {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ninigi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ninigi|ja|瓊瓊杵命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninigi|qq=}} rejected Iwanaga-hime, causing her to retreat to Kumomi, leading to enmity between the once-close sisters. According to [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]]'s ''Kojikiden,'' the jealous Iwanaga-hime fled to Kumomi, where she was enshrined on a small rocky mountain, while her kind sister stretched tall to look for her, becoming the towering Mount Fuji. Local legends say that when Mount Eboshi is clear, Mount Fuji is shrouded in clouds, and vice versa. Praising Mount Fuji from Mount Eboshi is said to invite misfortune, and "Fuji" was once taboo in local speech. == References == * ''Izu Encyclopedia,'' edited by the Izu Studies Association, Hagoromo Publishing, 2010. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == See Also == {{Commonscat|Kumomi-Sengen-jinja}} * [[Shrine Ranking system]] * {{ill|Mount Eboshi (Matsuzaki, Shizuoka)|ja|烏帽子山 (松崎町)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Eboshi (Matsuzaki, Shizuoka)|qq=}} * [[:en:Mount Ōmuro (Shizuoka)|Mount Ōmuro (Shizuoka)]] - Another Asama Shrine dedicated solely to Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto == External Links == * [https://www.town.matsuzaki.shizuoka.jp/docs/2020120700015/ Sengen Shrine] - Matsuzaki Town * [http://www.fujisan-jinja.com/shizuoka/kumomi_sengen/ Kumomi Sengen Shrine] - Shrine of Mount Fuji {{Shinto navbox}} {{Iwanaga Faith}} {{translated page|ja|雲見浅間神社|version=103489865|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Top level categories]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Matsuzaki, Shizuoka]] [[Category:History of Matsuzaki, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Asama shrines]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Iwanaga-hime]] }} [[ja:雲見浅間神社]] [[en:Kumomi Sengen Shrine]] [[sn:Kumomi Sengen Shrine]] [[Category:松崎町の建築物]]

After the abolition of the shrine Ranking system post-[[World War II]], local residents have maintained the shrine. == Legends == {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|lt=Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫尊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanaga-hime|qq=}}, the enshrined deity, is the sister of {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花咲耶姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|qq=}}, the deity of [[Mount Fuji]] and the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|Fujisan Hongŭ Sengen Taisha]], and daughter of {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山祗命|1=Ōyamatsumi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi|qq=}}. In mythology, Iwanaga-hime was considered unattractive but promised longevity like a rock to her offspring, while her beautiful sister Konohanasakuya-hime’s children were destined for short lives like flowers. The heavenly grandson {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ninigi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ninigi|ja|瓊瓊杵命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninigi|qq=}} rejected Iwanaga-hime, causing her to retreat to Kumomi, leading to enmity between the once-close sisters. According to [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]]'s ''Kojikiden,'' the jealous Iwanaga-hime fled to Kumomi, where she was enshrined on a small rocky mountain, while her kind sister stretched tall to look for her, becoming the towering Mount Fuji. Local legends say that when Mount Eboshi is clear, Mount Fuji is shrouded in clouds, and vice versa. Praising Mount Fuji from Mount Eboshi is said to invite misfortune, and "Fuji" was once taboo in local speech. == References == * ''Izu Encyclopedia,'' edited by the Izu Studies Association, Hagoromo Publishing, 2010. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == See Also == {{Commonscat|Kumomi-Sengen-jinja}} * [[Shrine Ranking system]] * {{ill|Mount Eboshi (Matsuzaki, Shizuoka)|ja|烏帽子山 (松崎町)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Eboshi (Matsuzaki, Shizuoka)|qq=}} * [[:en:Mount Ōmuro (Shizuoka)|Mount Ōmuro (Shizuoka)]] - Another Asama Shrine dedicated solely to Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto == External Links == * [https://www.town.matsuzaki.shizuoka.jp/docs/2020120700015/ Sengen Shrine] - Matsuzaki Town * [http://www.fujisan-jinja.com/shizuoka/kumomi_sengen/ Kumomi Sengen Shrine] - Shrine of Mount Fuji {{Shinto navbox}} {{Iwanaga Faith}} {{translated page|ja|雲見浅間神社|version=103489865|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Top level categories]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Matsuzaki, Shizuoka]] [[Category:History of Matsuzaki, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Asama shrines]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Iwanaga-hime]] }} [[ja:雲見浅間神社]] [[en:Kumomi Sengen Shrine]] [[sn:Kumomi Sengen Shrine]] [[Category:松崎町の建築物]] [[Category:History of Matsuzaki]] {{Commons category|Kumomi-Sengen-jinja}}

{{Unreferenced|date=2018-08-30}} {{Page number|date=2018-08-30}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name=Kumomi Sengen Shrine | image=[[File:Kumomi-Sengen-jinja (Honden).jpg|250px|Main Hall of Kumomi Sengen Shrine (Photographed on February 11, 2017)]]
Main Hall of Kumomi Sengen Shrine
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|coord={{coord|34|43|31.7|N|138|44|24.5|E}}}} | location=[[Shizuoka Prefecture]][[Kamo District, Shizuoka|Kamo District]][[Matsuzaki, Shizuoka|Matsuzaki Town]] Kumomi 386-2 | Latitude = 34 | Latitude_min = 43 | Latitude_sec = 31.7 | N/S = N | Longitude = 138 | Longitude_min = 44 | Longitude_sec = 24.5 | E/W = E | ISO = JP-22 | Map = Japan Shizuoka | Label Position = left | deity=[[Iwanaga-hime|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto]] | Shrine Rank=[[Gōsha]], (Traditionally) [[Shikinaisha]] | Established=Unknown | Annual Festival=[[July 14]], [[July 15]], [[July 16]] }} '''Kumomi Sengen Shrine''' (雲見浅間神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located on {{ill|Mount Eboshi (Matsuzaki, Shizuoka)|ja|烏帽子山 (松崎町)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Eboshi (Matsuzaki, Shizuoka)|qq=}}, with an elevation of 162 meters, in {{ill|Kumomi Onsen|ja|雲見温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumomi Onsen|qq=}}, [[:en:Matsuzaki, Shizuoka|Matsuzaki, Shizuoka]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan. It is also known as '''Kumomi Shrine''' (雲見神社) or '''Ontake Sengen Shrine''' (御嶽浅間宮). It is one of the rare [[Sengen shrine|Asama Shrines]] dedicated solely to {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|lt=Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫尊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanaga-hime|qq=}}. == Overview == Mount Eboshi, also called Ontake (御嶽山) or Kumomi-ga-take (雲見ヶ嶽), is a stand-alone [[:en:Tuff|Tuff]] rock formation located at the westernmost coast of the Izu Peninsula. The shrine's main hall is located just below the summit, while the worship hall and the middle shrine (Nakanomiya) are on the mountain's slopes. Historically, the shrine was off-limits to women, who were only allowed to worship at the middle shrine. From the base at [[:en:Japan National Route 136|National Route 136 (Japan)]], the shrine is accessible by a steep 25-minute climb, passing two worship halls before reaching the main shrine. The 428-step stone stairway is extremely steep and includes sections with unstable footing. Due to strong winds near the summit, visitors require sturdy footwear, and light clothing such as high heels is strongly discouraged. Kumomi-Sengen-jinja (Honden s3).jpg|Honden Kumomi-Sengen-jinja (Haiden s2).jpg|Haiden Kumomi-Sengen-jinja (Nakanomiya).jpg|Nakanomiya Meotomatsu Kumomi-Sengen-jinja.jpg|Sacred [[pine tree]] known as "Meoto Matsu" == History == The founding date and origins are unknown. It is traditionally identified with "Ishito no Myojin," listed as "Conferred Fourth Rank, Upper Grade" in the ''Izu Province Shrine Ranking Book,'' and believed to correspond to "Iwanohime no Mikoto Shrine" in the ''Engishiki Jinmyōchō,'' although conclusive evidence is lacking. According to the ''Zushu Shiko,'' the name "Ishito no Myojin" derives from a stone gate called "Asama-mon" in the sea, likely referring to the '''Senganmon''' rock formation about 800 meters south of Mount Eboshi. Shrine lore recounts that the main hall was swept into the sea by a typhoon and rebuilt in [[1657]] (Meireki 3). The shrine was a center for [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism) and visited by ascetics until the [[Meiji Restoration]] when it was designated as a [[Gōsha]] following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism. After the abolition of the shrine Ranking system post-[[World War II]], local residents have maintained the shrine. == Legends == {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|lt=Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫尊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanaga-hime|qq=}}, the enshrined deity, is the sister of {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花咲耶姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|qq=}}, the deity of [[Mount Fuji]] and the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|Fujisan Hongŭ Sengen Taisha]], and daughter of {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山祗命|1=Ōyamatsumi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi|qq=}}. In mythology, Iwanaga-hime was considered unattractive but promised longevity like a rock to her offspring, while her beautiful sister Konohanasakuya-hime’s children were destined for short lives like flowers. The heavenly grandson {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ninigi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ninigi|ja|瓊瓊杵命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninigi|qq=}} rejected Iwanaga-hime, causing her to retreat to Kumomi, leading to enmity between the once-close sisters. According to [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]]'s ''Kojikiden,'' the jealous Iwanaga-hime fled to Kumomi, where she was enshrined on a small rocky mountain, while her kind sister stretched tall to look for her, becoming the towering Mount Fuji. Local legends say that when Mount Eboshi is clear, Mount Fuji is shrouded in clouds, and vice versa. Praising Mount Fuji from Mount Eboshi is said to invite misfortune, and "Fuji" was once taboo in local speech. == References == * ''Izu Encyclopedia,'' edited by the Izu Studies Association, Hagoromo Publishing, 2010. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == See Also == {{Commonscat|Kumomi-Sengen-jinja}} * [[Shrine Ranking system]] * {{ill|Mount Eboshi (Matsuzaki, Shizuoka)|ja|烏帽子山 (松崎町)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Eboshi (Matsuzaki, Shizuoka)|qq=}} * [[:en:Mount Ōmuro (Shizuoka)|Mount Ōmuro (Shizuoka)]] - Another Asama Shrine dedicated solely to Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto == External Links == * [https://www.town.matsuzaki.shizuoka.jp/docs/2020120700015/ Sengen Shrine] - Matsuzaki Town * [http://www.fujisan-jinja.com/shizuoka/kumomi_sengen/ Kumomi Sengen Shrine] - Shrine of Mount Fuji {{Shinto navbox}} {{Iwanaga Faith}} {{translated page|ja|雲見浅間神社|version=103489865|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Top level categories]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Matsuzaki, Shizuoka]] [[Category:History of Matsuzaki, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Asama shrines]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Iwanaga-hime]] }} [[ja:雲見浅間神社]] [[sn:Kumomi Sengen Shrine]] [[Category:松崎町の建築物]] [[Category:History of Matsuzaki]] {{Commons category|Kumomi-Sengen-jinja}}

The 428-step stone stairway is extremely steep and includes sections with unstable footing. Due to strong winds near the summit, visitors require sturdy footwear, and light clothing such as high heels is strongly discouraged. Kumomi-Sengen-jinja (Honden s3).jpg|Honden Kumomi-Sengen-jinja (Haiden s2).jpg|Haiden Kumomi-Sengen-jinja (Nakanomiya).jpg|Nakanomiya Meotomatsu Kumomi-Sengen-jinja.jpg|Sacred [[pine tree]] known as "Meoto Matsu" == History == The founding date and origins are unknown. {{Unreferenced|date=2018-08-30}} {{Page number|date=2018-08-30}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name=Kumomi Sengen Shrine | image=[[File:Kumomi-Sengen-jinja (Honden).jpg|250px|Main Hall of Kumomi Sengen Shrine (Photographed on February 11, 2017)]]
Main Hall of Kumomi Sengen Shrine
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|coord={{coord|34|43|31.7|N|138|44|24.5|E}}}} | location=[[Shizuoka Prefecture]][[Kamo District, Shizuoka|Kamo District]][[Matsuzaki, Shizuoka|Matsuzaki Town]] Kumomi 386-2 | Latitude = 34 | Latitude_min = 43 | Latitude_sec = 31.7 | N/S = N | Longitude = 138 | Longitude_min = 44 | Longitude_sec = 24.5 | E/W = E | ISO = JP-22 | Map = Japan Shizuoka | Label Position = left | deity=[[Iwanaga-hime|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto]] | Shrine Rank=[[Gōsha]], (Traditionally) [[Shikinaisha]] | Established=Unknown | Annual Festival=[[July 14]], [[July 15]], [[July 16]] }} '''Kumomi Sengen Shrine''' (雲見浅間神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located on {{ill|Mount Eboshi (Matsuzaki, Shizuoka)|ja|烏帽子山 (松崎町)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Eboshi (Matsuzaki, Shizuoka)|qq=}}, with an elevation of 162 meters, in {{ill|Kumomi Onsen|ja|雲見温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumomi Onsen|qq=}}, [[:en:Matsuzaki, Shizuoka|Matsuzaki, Shizuoka]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan. It is also known as '''Kumomi Shrine''' (雲見神社) or '''Ontake Sengen Shrine''' (御嶽浅間宮). It is one of the rare [[Sengen shrine|Asama Shrines]] dedicated solely to {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|lt=Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫尊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanaga-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. == Overview == Mount Eboshi, also called Ontake (御嶽山) or Kumomi-ga-take (雲見ヶ嶽), is a stand-alone [[:en:Tuff|Tuff]] rock formation located at the westernmost coast of the Izu Peninsula. The shrine's main hall is located just below the summit, while the worship hall and the middle shrine (Nakanomiya) are on the mountain's slopes. Historically, the shrine was off-limits to women, who were only allowed to worship at the middle shrine. From the base at [[:en:Japan National Route 136|National Route 136 (Japan)]], the shrine is accessible by a steep 25-minute climb, passing two worship halls before reaching the main shrine. The 428-step stone stairway is extremely steep and includes sections with unstable footing. Due to strong winds near the summit, visitors require sturdy footwear, and light clothing such as high heels is strongly discouraged. Kumomi-Sengen-jinja (Honden s3).jpg|Honden Kumomi-Sengen-jinja (Haiden s2).jpg|Haiden Kumomi-Sengen-jinja (Nakanomiya).jpg|Nakanomiya Meotomatsu Kumomi-Sengen-jinja.jpg|Sacred [[pine tree]] known as "Meoto Matsu" == History == The founding date and origins are unknown. It is traditionally identified with "Ishito no Myojin," listed as "Conferred Fourth Rank, Upper Grade" in the ''Izu Province Shrine Ranking Book,'' and believed to correspond to "Iwanohime no Mikoto Shrine" in the ''Engishiki Jinmyōchō,'' although conclusive evidence is lacking. According to the ''Zushu Shiko,'' the name "Ishito no Myojin" derives from a stone gate called "Asama-mon" in the sea, likely referring to the '''Senganmon''' rock formation about 800 meters south of Mount Eboshi. Shrine lore recounts that the main hall was swept into the sea by a typhoon and rebuilt in [[:en:1657|1657]] (Meireki 3). The shrine was a center for [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism) and visited by ascetics until the [[Meiji Restoration]] when it was designated as a [[Gōsha]] following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism. After the abolition of the shrine Ranking system post-[[World War II]], local residents have maintained the shrine. == Legends == {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|lt=Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫尊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanaga-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the enshrined deity, is the sister of {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花咲耶姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}}, the deity of [[Mount Fuji]] and the [[Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|Fujisan Hongŭ Sengen Taisha]], and daughter of {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山祗命|1=Ōyamatsumi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオヤマツミ}}. In mythology, Iwanaga-hime was considered unattractive but promised longevity like a rock to her offspring, while her beautiful sister Konohanasakuya-hime’s children were destined for short lives like flowers. The heavenly grandson {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ninigi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ninigi|ja|瓊瓊杵命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninigi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ニニギ}} rejected Iwanaga-hime, causing her to retreat to Kumomi, leading to enmity between the once-close sisters. According to [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]]'s ''Kojikiden,'' the jealous Iwanaga-hime fled to Kumomi, where she was enshrined on a small rocky mountain, while her kind sister stretched tall to look for her, becoming the towering Mount Fuji. Local legends say that when Mount Eboshi is clear, Mount Fuji is shrouded in clouds, and vice versa. Praising Mount Fuji from Mount Eboshi is said to invite misfortune, and "Fuji" was once taboo in local speech. == References == * ''Izu Encyclopedia,'' edited by the Izu Studies Association, Hagoromo Publishing, 2010. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == See Also == {{Commonscat|Kumomi-Sengen-jinja}} * [[Shrine Ranking system]] * {{ill|Mount Eboshi (Matsuzaki, Shizuoka)|ja|烏帽子山 (松崎町)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Eboshi (Matsuzaki, Shizuoka)|qq=}} * [[:en:Mount Ōmuro (Shizuoka)|Mount Ōmuro (Shizuoka)]] - Another Asama Shrine dedicated solely to Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto == External Links == * [https://www.town.matsuzaki.shizuoka.jp/docs/2020120700015/ Sengen Shrine] - Matsuzaki Town * [http://www.fujisan-jinja.com/shizuoka/kumomi_sengen/ Kumomi Sengen Shrine] - Shrine of Mount Fuji {{Shinto navbox}} {{Iwanaga Faith}} {{translated page|ja|雲見浅間神社|version=103489865|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Top level categories]] [[Category:History of Matsuzaki, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Asama shrines]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Iwanaga-hime]] }} [[ja:雲見浅間神社]] [[sn:Kumomi Sengen Shrine]] [[Category:松崎町の建築物]] [[Category:History of Matsuzaki]] {{Commons category|Kumomi-Sengen-jinja}}

Due to strong winds near the summit, visitors require sturdy footwear, and light clothing such as high heels is strongly discouraged.
